edges.description: Loss of proper GCDH assembly decreases catalytic function in lysine degradation.
edges.target: GCDH enzymatic deficiency and disrupted lysine catabolism

edges.description: GCDH deficiency produces elevated blood glutarylcarnitine, the primary acylcarnitine marker used for newborn screening.
edges.source: GCDH enzymatic deficiency and disrupted lysine catabolism

edges.description: Impaired GCDH-dependent lysine catabolism causes urinary glutaric acid accumulation.
edges.source: GCDH enzymatic deficiency and disrupted lysine catabolism

edges.description: Disrupted GCDH-dependent catabolism also elevates urinary 3-hydroxyglutaric acid, a diagnostic GA1 metabolite.
edges.source: GCDH enzymatic deficiency and disrupted lysine catabolism

edges.description: Historical model proposes predominant local production of toxic catabolites within the brain.
intermediate_mechanisms: local lysine catabolic flux in brain tissue
edges.source: GCDH enzymatic deficiency and disrupted lysine catabolism
edges.target: Brain exposure to toxic GA1 catabolites

edges.description: Alternative model proposes hepatic generation with transport of toxic catabolites to brain.
edges.source: GCDH enzymatic deficiency and disrupted lysine catabolism
edges.target: Brain exposure to toxic GA1 catabolites

edges.description: The GA1 neurologic presentation often includes progressive macrocephaly in the same diagnostic context as characteristic brain MRI abnormalities.
edges.source: Brain exposure to toxic GA1 catabolites

edges.description: Toxic metabolite exposure perturbs redox balance and activates inflammatory pathways.
edges.source: Brain exposure to toxic GA1 catabolites

edges.description: Toxic metabolite burden precipitates acute encephalopathy with selective striatal damage during stress.
edges.source: Brain exposure to toxic GA1 catabolites

edges.description: Chronic toxic-metabolite burden, especially the biochemical high-excreter state, is associated with cognitive impairment despite newborn-screening-era treatment.
edges.source: Brain exposure to toxic GA1 catabolites

edges.description: Redox and inflammatory injury amplify selective striatal degeneration.
Acute striatal degeneration manifests clinically as encephalopathic crises.
Acute encephalopathic crises commonly include hypotonia as an early neurologic manifestation.
Neurologic injury and infantile GA1 presentation are associated with loss of motor skills and frequent motor developmental delay.
Bilateral striatal injury produces chronic dystonic movement disorder.
Seizures may occur as part of the acute encephalopathic crisis phenotype.
Progressive striatal/cortical injury contributes to chronic cerebral atrophic changes.
Chronic cerebral atrophy and expanded CSF spaces in GA1 stretch cortical veins and predispose to subdural hematoma.
intermediate_mechanisms: cerebral atrophy and expansion of CSF spaces

edges.description: White-matter microstructural injury accompanies gray-matter basal ganglia pathology.
edges.target: Cerebral white matter hyperintensity on MRI

edges.description: Cerebral atrophy and expanded CSF spaces stretch cortical veins and predispose to subdural hematoma.

nodes.description: The pan-PPAR agonist bezafibrate has shown neuroprotective effects in GCDH-deficient mice by normalizing oxidative stress and neuroinflammation in the striatum.

evidence.reference_title: Disturbance of Mitochondrial Dynamics, Endoplasmic Reticulum-Mitochondria Crosstalk, Redox Homeostasis, and Inflammatory Response in the Brain of Glutaryl-CoA Dehydrogenase-Deficient Mice: Neuroprotective Effects of Bezafibrate.
evidence.snippet: in vivo treatment with the pan-PPAR agonist bezafibrate normalized these alterations.

evidence.reference_title: Disturbance of Mitochondrial Dynamics, Endoplasmic Reticulum-Mitochondria Crosstalk, Redox Homeostasis, and Inflammatory Response in the Brain of Glutaryl-CoA Dehydrogenase-Deficient Mice: Neuroprotective Effects of Bezafibrate.
evidence.snippet: bezafibrate should be tested as a potential adjuvant therapy for GA1.

nodes.description: Brain tissue exposure to GA, 3-OH-GA, and related metabolites is the convergent toxic step in GA1. Competing origin models (intracerebral production versus hepatic source with transport) can be superimposed at this node.
nodes.id: Brain exposure to toxic GA1 catabolites

evidence.reference_title: Systemic delivery of AAV-GCDH ameliorates HLD-induced phenotype in a glutaric aciduria type I mouse model.
evidence.snippet: a lack of treatment on an HLD triggers very high accumulation of glutaric acid, 3-hydroxyglutaric acid, and glutarylcarnitine in tissues, with about 60% death due to brain accumulation of toxic lysine metabolites.

evidence.reference_title: Rescue of glutaric aciduria type I in mice by liver-directed therapies.
evidence.snippet: the characteristic brain and lethal phenotype of the GA-1 mouse model was rescued by two different liver-directed gene therapy approaches

nodes.description: L-carnitine supplementation helps replenish secondary carnitine deficiency caused by urinary losses of glutarylcarnitine (C5DC) and supports metabolite detoxification.

evidence.snippet: Combined metabolic therapy includes low-lysine diet, carnitine supplementation, and emergency treatment during episodes with the goal of averting catabolism and minimizing CNS exposure to lysine and its toxic metabolic byproducts.

nodes.description: Abnormalities in white matter are detected on MRI in the majority of GA1 patients.
nodes.id: Cerebral white matter hyperintensity on MRI

evidence.reference_title: Glutaric Aciduria Type 1: Comparison between Diffusional Kurtosis Imaging and Conventional MR Imaging.
evidence.snippet: Diffusional kurtosis imaging provides more comprehensive quantitative information regarding the gray and white matter micropathologic damage in glutaric aciduria type 1 than routine MR imaging scores.

nodes.description: Complex dystonic movement disorder resulting from bilateral striatal injury during encephalopathic crises. Severity correlates with extent of putamen damage on neuroimaging.

evidence.reference_title: Glutaric Aciduria Type 1: Comparison between Diffusional Kurtosis Imaging and Conventional MR Imaging.
evidence.snippet: The diffusional kurtosis imaging metrics of the temporal lobe and basal ganglia were significantly correlated with the Barry-Albright dystonia scores.

evidence.snippet: GA (94.37%) and 3OHGA (69.39%) in urine

readouts.interpretation: Elevated C5DC in blood reports the disrupted GCDH-dependent lysine-catabolism pathway.

nodes.description: Aggressive emergency treatment during catabolic crises including high-energy intravenous glucose, prevention of catabolism, and monitoring to prevent acute striatal necrosis. The emergency protocol is critical during the vulnerability window (3-36 months of age).

evidence.snippet: Combined metabolic therapy includes low-lysine diet, carnitine supplementation, and emergency treatment during episodes with the goal of averting catabolism and minimizing CNS exposure to lysine and its toxic metabolic byproducts.

Agents/circumstances to avoid: Excessive dietary protein or protein malnutrition inducing catabolic state, prolonged fasting, catabolic illness (intercurrent infection; brief febrile illness post vaccination), inadequate caloric provision during other stressors (e.g., surgery or procedure requiring fasting/anesthesia).

nodes.description: Acute encephalopathic crises typically occur between 3 and 36 months of age, often triggered by catabolic stress from febrile illness. These crises cause irreversible striatal injury if not prevented by emergency management.

evidence.reference_title: Disturbance of Mitochondrial Dynamics, Endoplasmic Reticulum-Mitochondria Crosstalk, Redox Homeostasis, and Inflammatory Response in the Brain of Glutaryl-CoA Dehydrogenase-Deficient Mice: Neuroprotective Effects of Bezafibrate.
evidence.snippet: commonly manifest acute encephalopathy associated with severe striatum degeneration

nodes.description: Frontotemporal atrophy with widened Sylvian fissures and expanded CSF spaces is a characteristic neuroimaging finding in GA1.

evidence.reference_title: Subdural hematomas: glutaric aciduria type 1 or abusive head trauma? A systematic review.
evidence.snippet: cerebral atrophy and expansion of CSF spaces

nodes.description: Deficiency of glutaryl-CoA dehydrogenase (GCDH), the last enzyme of lysine catabolism, drives accumulation of glutaric acid (GA), 3-hydroxyglutaric acid (3-OH-GA), and glutarylcarnitine (C5DC).
nodes.id: GCDH enzymatic deficiency and disrupted lysine catabolism

evidence.reference_title: Rescue of glutaric aciduria type I in mice by liver-directed therapies.
evidence.snippet: Glutaric aciduria type I (GA-1) is an inborn error of metabolism with a severe neurological phenotype caused by the deficiency of glutaryl-coenzyme A dehydrogenase (GCDH), the last enzyme of lysine catabolism.

evidence.reference_title: Glutaryl-CoA Dehydrogenase Misfolding in Glutaric Acidemia Type 1.
evidence.snippet: Glutaric acidemia type 1 (GA1) is a neurotoxic metabolic disorder due to glutaryl-CoA dehydrogenase (GCDH) deficiency.

nodes.description: Many GA1-causing missense variants lead to protein misfolding characterized by altered oligomerization, loss of protein stability and solubility, and increased susceptibility to aggregation. Reduced cellular activity is associated with loss of GCDH tetramerization. Variants closer to the N-terminus show more pronounced protein loss.

evidence.reference_title: Glutaryl-CoA Dehydrogenase Misfolding in Glutaric Acidemia Type 1.
evidence.snippet: An altered oligomerization, loss of protein stability and solubility, as well as an augmented susceptibility to aggregation were observed.

evidence.reference_title: Glutaryl-CoA Dehydrogenase Misfolding in Glutaric Acidemia Type 1.
evidence.snippet: The reduced cellular activity was associated with loss of tetramerization.

evidence.reference_title: Glutaryl-CoA Dehydrogenase Misfolding in Glutaric Acidemia Type 1.
evidence.snippet: The broad panel of variant-mediated conformational changes of the GCDH protein supports the classification of GA1 as a protein-misfolding disorder.

nodes.id: GCDH variants causing glutaryl-CoA dehydrogenase deficiency

evidence.reference_title: Clinical features and GCDH gene variants in three Chinese families with glutaric aciduria type 1: A case series and literature review.
evidence.snippet: 67 distinct GCDH gene variants were identified among 73 patients, with missense variants being the most prevalent type (73.97%). The most frequent variant was c.1244-2 A > C, observed in 17.12% of cases.

evidence.reference_title: Glutaryl-CoA Dehydrogenase Misfolding in Glutaric Acidemia Type 1.
evidence.snippet: The high number of missense variants associated with the disease and their impact on GCDH activity suggest that disturbed protein conformation can affect the biochemical phenotype.

evidence.reference_title: Clinical features and GCDH gene variants in three Chinese families with glutaric aciduria type 1: A case series and literature review.
evidence.snippet: the majority of variant sites were located in exons 11 (25.37%) and 6 (22.39%).

evidence.reference_title: GCDH / glutaryl-CoA dehydrogenase deficiency (Definitive)
evidence.snippet: GCDH | HGNC:4189 | glutaryl-CoA dehydrogenase deficiency | MONDO:0009281 | AR | Definitive

nodes.description: AAV-mediated gene therapy is under preclinical investigation for GA1. Both liver-directed approaches (replacing GCDH or deleting AASS to prevent lysine degradation flux) and systemic AAV9-GCDH delivery have shown efficacy in mouse models, restoring enzyme activity and preventing lysine diet-induced neuropathology.

evidence.reference_title: Rescue of glutaric aciduria type I in mice by liver-directed therapies.
evidence.snippet: the characteristic brain and lethal phenotype of the GA-1 mouse model was rescued by two different liver-directed gene therapy approaches: Using an adeno-associated virus, we replaced the defective Gcdh gene or we prevented flux through the lysine degradation pathway by CRISPR deletion of the aminoadipate-semialdehyde synthase (Aass) gene.

evidence.reference_title: Systemic delivery of AAV-GCDH ameliorates HLD-induced phenotype in a glutaric aciduria type I mouse model.
evidence.snippet: Neonatal treatment with AAV-GCDH restores GCDH expression and enzyme activity in liver and striatum.

evidence.reference_title: Systemic delivery of AAV-GCDH ameliorates HLD-induced phenotype in a glutaric aciduria type I mouse model.
evidence.snippet: AAV-GCDH significantly ameliorates the striatal neuropathology, minimizing neuronal dysfunction, gliosis, and alterations in myelination.

nodes.description: Genetic counseling is essential for families with GA1 to explain the autosomal recessive inheritance pattern, recurrence risk, and options for prenatal diagnosis.

evidence.reference_title: Clinical features and GCDH gene variants in three Chinese families with glutaric aciduria type 1: A case series and literature review.
evidence.snippet: These findings facilitate the diagnosis and treatment of affected children and provide a basis for genetic counseling and prenatal diagnosis for their families.
Evaluation of relatives at risk: Testing of all at-risk sibs of any age to allow for early diagnosis and treatment.

Once the GCDH pathogenic variants in an affected family member are known, carrier testing for at-risk relatives, prenatal testing for a pregnancy at increased risk, and preimplantation genetic testing are possible.

nodes.description: Muscular hypotonia may be present in infancy, particularly before the onset of dystonia.

Cognitive performance is mildly reduced in GA1 patients identified by newborn screening, particularly in those with the biochemical high excreter phenotype (median IQ 84) compared to low excreters (median IQ 98). Most patients do not meet criteria for intellectual disability (IQ <70) but have below-average cognitive function.

evidence.reference_title: The biochemical subtype is a predictor for cognitive function in glutaric aciduria type 1: a national prospective follow-up study.
evidence.snippet: The biochemical high excreter phenotype is the major risk factor for cognitive impairment while cognitive functions do not appear to be impacted by current therapy and striatal damage.

nodes.description: A low-lysine or lysine-free diet is a cornerstone of GA1 metabolic treatment, aiming to reduce substrate availability for the deficient enzyme and decrease toxic metabolite production.

evidence.reference_title: The biochemical subtype is a predictor for cognitive function in glutaric aciduria type 1: a national prospective follow-up study.
evidence.snippet: Long-term neurologic outcome in GA1 involves both motor and cognitive functions. The biochemical high excreter phenotype is the major risk factor for cognitive impairment while cognitive functions do not appear to be impacted by current therapy and striatal damage.

Combined metabolic therapy includes low-lysine diet, carnitine supplementation, and emergency treatment during episodes with the goal of averting catabolism and minimizing CNS exposure to lysine and its toxic metabolic byproducts.

nodes.description: Increased head circumference is one of the most common clinical manifestations, present in approximately 77% of patients. Often the first clinical sign raising suspicion before screening results are available.

evidence.reference_title: Clinical features and GCDH gene variants in three Chinese families with glutaric aciduria type 1: A case series and literature review.
evidence.snippet: The most common clinical manifestations included increased head circumference (77.19%)

nodes.description: Developmental motor delay is common, present in approximately 65% of patients. May be evident before or after an encephalopathic crisis.

evidence.reference_title: Clinical features and GCDH gene variants in three Chinese families with glutaric aciduria type 1: A case series and literature review.
evidence.snippet: motor developmental delay (65.15%)

nodes.description: Newborn screening (NBS) for GA1 using tandem mass spectrometry detection of elevated glutarylcarnitine (C5DC) enables presymptomatic diagnosis and early treatment initiation. Machine learning-based digital-tier strategies can reduce false-positive rates by over 90%.

evidence.reference_title: Digital-Tier Strategy Improves Newborn Screening for Glutaric Aciduria Type 1.
evidence.snippet: the proposed digital-tier strategy based on logistic regression analysis, ridge regression, and support vector machine reduced the false-positive rate by over 90% compared to regular NBS while identifying all confirmed individuals with GA1 correctly.

evidence.reference_title: Clinical features and GCDH gene variants in three Chinese families with glutaric aciduria type 1: A case series and literature review.
evidence.snippet: 38.36% were diagnosed through newborn screening
Because the early initiation of treatment dramatically improved the outcome for persons with GA-1, an international guideline group has recommended NBS.

nodes.description: GCDH deficiency leads to disturbed redox homeostasis including increased lipid peroxidation, altered antioxidant defenses, and a pro-inflammatory response in the striatum and cerebral cortex. NF-kB pathway activation and microglial activation contribute to neuroinflammation. Mitochondrial dynamics are also disrupted with activated mitochondrial fission.

evidence.reference_title: Disturbance of Mitochondrial Dynamics, Endoplasmic Reticulum-Mitochondria Crosstalk, Redox Homeostasis, and Inflammatory Response in the Brain of Glutaryl-CoA Dehydrogenase-Deficient Mice: Neuroprotective Effects of Bezafibrate.
evidence.snippet: Increased lipid peroxidation and altered antioxidant defenses, including decreased concentrations of reduced glutathione and increased activities of superoxide dismutase, catalase, and glutathione transferase, were observed in the striatum and cerebral cortex of Gcdh-/- mice.

evidence.reference_title: Disturbance of Mitochondrial Dynamics, Endoplasmic Reticulum-Mitochondria Crosstalk, Redox Homeostasis, and Inflammatory Response in the Brain of Glutaryl-CoA Dehydrogenase-Deficient Mice: Neuroprotective Effects of Bezafibrate.
evidence.snippet: the nuclear content of NF-κB was increased, and the cytosolic content of IκBα decreased in the striatum of the mutant animals, indicating a pro-inflammatory response.

nodes.description: Structure-targeted allosteric regulators are being explored as pharmacological chaperones for GA1 protein-misfolding variants, aiming to stabilize folded GCDH and increase residual enzyme function without competing with the natural substrate.

evidence.reference_title: Use of the Novel Site-Directed Enzyme Enhancement Therapy (SEE-Tx) Drug Discovery Platform to Identify Pharmacological Chaperones for Glutaric Acidemia Type 1.
evidence.snippet: Putative allosteric regulators were discovered using structure- and ligand-based virtual screening methods and validated using orthogonal biophysical and biochemical assays.

nodes.description: Seizures may occur during or after encephalopathic crises.

The immature striatum is selectively vulnerable to damage during acute encephalopathic crises, typically occurring between ages 3 and 36 months. Catabolic stress from intercurrent illness triggers massive accumulation of neurotoxic metabolites, leading to bilateral striatal necrosis. This results in an irreversible complex dystonic movement disorder. The vulnerability window corresponds to a critical developmental period of striatal maturation.

evidence.reference_title: Disturbance of Mitochondrial Dynamics, Endoplasmic Reticulum-Mitochondria Crosstalk, Redox Homeostasis, and Inflammatory Response in the Brain of Glutaryl-CoA Dehydrogenase-Deficient Mice: Neuroprotective Effects of Bezafibrate.
evidence.snippet: commonly manifest acute encephalopathy associated with severe striatum degeneration and progressive cortical and striatal injury

evidence.reference_title: Glutaric Aciduria Type 1: Comparison between Diffusional Kurtosis Imaging and Conventional MR Imaging.
evidence.snippet: The mean kurtosis values of the anterior and posterior putamen and Barry-Albright dystonia scores were most relevant (r = 0.721, 0.730, respectively).
crises result in acute bilateral striatal injury and subsequent complex movement disorders.

nodes.description: Children with GA1 are predisposed to subdural hematoma development due to stretching of cortical veins secondary to cerebral atrophy and expansion of CSF spaces. SDH in GA1 must be distinguished from abusive head trauma.

evidence.reference_title: Subdural hematomas: glutaric aciduria type 1 or abusive head trauma? A systematic review.
evidence.snippet: Children with GA1 are reported to be predisposed to subdural hematoma (SDH) development due to stretching of cortical veins secondary to cerebral atrophy and expansion of CSF spaces.

evidence.reference_title: Subdural hematomas: glutaric aciduria type 1 or abusive head trauma? A systematic review.
evidence.snippet: SDHs in 19/20 children with GA1 are accompanied by other brain abnormalities specific for GA1.
